data.title: Rove: Neither Newsom, Pritzker can claim 'solid record of economic achievement'
data.description:
Republican strategist Karl Rove on Thursday wrote that California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) and Illinois Gov. JB Pritzker (D), both possible 2028 presidential contenders, cannot claim a “solid record of economic achievement.”

“Mr. Newsom has great hair and Mr. Pritzker a vast fortune,” Rove wrote in an op-ed published in The Wall Street Journal. “But neither will matter nearly as much as their records as governor. Neither man can credibly claim that he has a solid record of economic achievement. That may not matter much to Democratic primary voters. It will in November 2028.”

Rove referred to Newsom’s attendance at the Munich Security Conference last week, when the governor spoke out against President Trump and said he was “temporary.” The strategist wrote that while history will judge Trump’s presidency, Newsom’s record “will have immediate, electoral consequences if he tries to succeed Mr. Trump.”

Rove noted that Newsom is distinct for being the first California governor since its statehood in 1850 to see the state’s congressional delegation decrease. He cited U.S. Census Bureau data showing a decline in the state’s population between April 2020 and July 2025.

Bureau data shows that the population slightly dropped from 39,555,703 as of April 1, 2020, to 39,355,309 by July 1, 2025.

Rove also referred to California being ranked at No. 32 for its economy in U.S. News & World Report’s “Best States” ranking, followed by 42 on fiscal stability, 45 on growth, 46 on employment, “and dead last for opportunity.” He knocked Newsom’s “stewardship” for the Golden State’s Central Valley High-Speed Rail project, where testing of the initial rail line is scheduled for 2028, according to the California High Speed Rail Authority.

“Mr. Newsom’s only comfort is that he wouldn’t be the only Democratic hopeful with home-state problems to overcome,” Rove wrote. “Under Gov. JB Pritzker, Illinois is, in many ways, also a failing state. In the U.S. News ‘Best State’ rankings, it was No. 35 for opportunity, 42 for the economy and last for long-term fiscal stability.”

Rover cited the departures of companies like Boeing and Caterpillar, which he called “more than an economic hit and less opportunity for the state’s people.”

“It also reduces Illinois’s political clout,” he continued. “The Prairie State had 18 representatives in 2018, when Mr. Pritzker was elected. That number is down to 17 and is expected to be 16 after the 2030 census.”

Both governors have boasted about their respective state’s economic successes. Pritzker on Wednesday revealed his eighth balanced budget proposal, “a fiscally responsible plan centered on making Illinois more affordable for working families while protecting the state’s long-term financial stability,” his office said in a statement.

The new budget relies on “seven years of restored fiscal stability” in improving credit ratings and lowering the state’s bill backlog, and ensures “Illinois can continue delivering results while keeping costs down for residents.”

Newsom last year praised California for being the fourth-largest economy in the world, behind the U.S., China and Germany. He warned that Trump’s tariff policy could hurt the state’s standing as an economic powerhouse.

“While we celebrate this success, we recognize that our progress is threatened by the reckless tariff policies of the current federal administration,” Newsom said in a statement from April 2025. “California’s economy powers the nation, and it must be protected.”

data.title: Moore on Trump: 'I won't bow to anybody'
data.description:
Maryland Gov. Wes Moore in an interview Thursday with Blake Burman, the host of NewsNation’s “The Hill,” said he’s willing to work with President Trump in the interest of his state but he “won’t bow to anybody.”

Moore’s comments come amid a emerging battles between Trump and the Maryland governor, broadly seen as a rising Democratic star, over issues as disparate as a sewage spill in the Potomac River and the president’s efforts to block him from attending a National Governors Association’s (NGA) event at the White House.

Asked by Burman about how he views the balance between working with Trump and pushing back when he feels he needs to, Moore said his obligation was to his state.

“My obligation, and what I’ve always told the people of my state is that I will work with anybody … 97 percent of legislation I’ve introduced as governor, that has passed, has been bipartisan in the way that it’s been passed. But I won’t bow to anybody,” he said.

Trump has excluded Moore and Democratic Gov. Jared Polis (D) from the NGA’s upcoming dinner at the White House this Saturday. 

Moore has clashed repeatedly with the Trump administration during his governorship, most recently exchanging blame with the president over a significant sewage spill in the Potomac River, which runs through his state and Washington, D.C.

Trump pinned the “massive Ecological Disaster” on the Maryland governor in a Truth Social post, accusing Moore and other state Democratic officials of “Gross Mismanagement.”

All other Republican and Democratic governors received invitations to the black-tie event. Moore and Polis were invited to the NGA’s meeting at the White House on Friday. 

Moore hit back at the president’s decision to bar him from the dinner in a statement, alleging racial discrimination from the president. 

“As the nation’s only Black governor, I can’t ignore that being singled out for exclusion from this bipartisan tradition carries an added weight — whether that was the intent or not,” he said. 

Democratic Governors Association Chair Gov. Andy Beshear (D-Ky.) said all 18 Democratic governors intend to skip any White House event this weekend unless all governors receive invitations. 

Beshear called the exclusions “just a lot of drama” and “unnecessary” in an interview last Sunday with CNN’s Jake Tapper. 

“It no longer looks like it’s going to be productive at all,” Beshear said. “At this point, I’m not going. If you’re not going to invite all the Democratic governors, this chair of the DGA isn’t going to be there.”

data.title: Live updates: Trump speaks in Georgia as DHS shutdown drags on
data.description:
President Trump is in Georgia, where he is delivering remarks about the economy.

His visit to the battleground state comes as Capitol Hill remains at a standstill on negotiations to fund the Department of Homeland Security (DHS). Funding lapsed for the department over the weekend.

The White House has taken aim at Democrats, slamming the party for not making concessions amid the impasse over tactics used by Immigration and Customs Enforcement to carry out Trump’s deportation agenda. However, specific details about the discussion have not surfaced, with lawmakers and aides pointing to the relative quiet as a sign that serious talks are taking place.

Earlier Thursday, dozens of world leaders gathered in Washington for the president’s inaugural Board of Peace meeting. 

The board, which was launched by Trump last month, aims to oversee the rehabilitation of the Gaza Strip amid a fragile Israel-Hamas ceasefire and after the war’s end. Several countries, including many U.S. allies, declined to participate over concerns of the board’s framework as well as member countries, including Russia and Belarus.

Meanwhile, the Jeffrey Epstein saga has intensified with the arrest of former Prince Andrew on suspicion of misconduct while in public office after the release of millions of pages of documents in late January.

The National Governors Association’s annual meeting will also begin Thursday as Trump digs in on his criticism of some Democratic leaders ahead of the 2026 midterm elections.

Follow live.

data.title: Pritzker: Americans still paying 'too much to live'
data.description:
Illinois Gov. JB Pritzker (D) attacked the Trump administration’s fiscal policies during his “State of the State” address on Wednesday, citing Illinois residents’ continued cost of living concerns. 

“We won’t let headwinds from Washington stop us from addressing the fact that Illinoisans, like Americans everywhere, are still paying too much for groceries, too much for housing, too much for electricity, too much to live,” the governor said. “Everything is just too damned expensive.”

The governor presented a balanced budget proposal to state lawmakers in Illinois’ General Assembly on Wednesday. The governor said the state took significant financial hits in attempting to comply with federal policies over the past year. 

“Despite the headwinds, the Illinois economy has proven remarkably resilient — forging ahead on our path toward accelerating growth and expansion,” Pritzker said. 

The governor’s remarks follow a tumultuous year in economic policy under President Trump, with consumers bearing significant costs resulting from tariffs on trade partners.

The Tax Foundation estimated this month that Trump’s tariffs have resulted in an average tax increase of $1,000 per US household in 2025.

The president has continued to defend these import taxes, writing in a Wall Street Journal op-ed in January that the “tariffs have brought America back.”

Pritzker accused the Trump administration of costing Illinois taxpayers a total of $8.4 billion and cited the state’s more than 50 cases accusing the federal government of “illegally confiscating” federal dollars allocated for Illinois. 

The governor also addressed the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown efforts in cities across the country. Federal agents conducted immigration enforcement operations in Chicago last year over the protests of state and local Democratic officials. 

The governor invoked a controversial moment during his annual address last year, in which Republican lawmakers walked out of the room after he broached the issue of discrimination against immigrants. 

“But a year later, we have an answer, don’t we?” he said on Wednesday. “Masked, unaccountable federal agents — with little training — occupied our streets, brutalized our people, tear gassed kids and cops, kidnapped parents in front of their children, detained and arrested and at times attempted to deport US citizens, and killed innocent Americans in the streets.”

The governor called Illinois the “canary in the coal mine” for the Trump administration’s operations in Minnesota, during which federal agents shot and killed two of the state’s residents. 

“It’s a playbook as old as the game: Overwhelm communities, provoke fear, suggest that those tasked with enforcing the law are also above it, and drip authoritarianism bit by bit into our veins in the hopes that we won’t notice we are being poisoned by it,” Pritzker said. “The problem for Donald Trump and Stephen Miller was that Illinoisans did notice.”

The governor closed his Wednesday remarks with a plea to his contemporaries and constituents to choose “empathy and kindness” over the alternative “cruelty and rage.”

“What you choose to arm yourself with in this fight, love or hate, exposes which side you are fighting on. Only the weakest of people believe that love is the weakest of weapons,” he said. “And it turns out that love actually is all around — and that those who think that cruelty can destroy it, are incapable of understanding the power of a nation moved by it.

“I love my country. I refuse to stop,” he continued. “The hope I have found in a very difficult year is that love is the light that gets you through a long night.”

data.title: Trump accuses Obama of revealing classified information on aliens
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday accused former President Obama of revealing classified information when weighing in on whether extraterrestrial life existed.

“Barack Obama said that aliens are real. Have you seen any evidence of non-human visitors to earth?” Fox News’ Peter Doocy asked Trump on Air Force One. 

“Well, he gave classified information. He’s not supposed to be doing that,” Trump responded. 

“So aliens are real?” Doocy followed up. 

“I don’t know if they’re real or not,” Trump responded. “I can tell you he gave classified information. He’s not supposed to be doing that. He made a big mistake. He took it out of classified information.” 

Trump went on to note that he does not have an opinion on whether aliens are real or not. 

“I never talk about it. A lot of people do,” the president said. “Do you believe it, Peter?”

“The president can declassify anything that he wants to,” Doocy responded. 

“I may get him out of trouble by declassifying,” Trump said during the exchange.

The question came in the wake of Obama being questioned about whether aliens are real In an interview published on Saturday on the “No Lie” podcast. 

“They’re real, but I haven’t seen them,” Obama responded before adding that they are not being kept in Area 51.

“There’s no underground facility, unless there’s this enormous conspiracy, and they hid it from the president of the United States,” he added.

Obama later clarified the comments in a social media post on Sunday, saying he “saw no evidence during my presidency that extraterrestrials have made contact with us.”

“I was trying to stick with the spirit of the speed round, but since it’s gotten attention let me clarify. Statistically, the universe is so vast that the odds are good there’s life out there,” Obama said. 

“But the distances between solar systems are so great that the chances we’ve been visited by aliens is low, and I saw no evidence during my presidency that extraterrestrials have made contact with us. Really!” he added.

data.title: Hegseth invited controversial Christian nationalist to preach at Pentagon
data.description:
A controversial Christian nationalist pastor who has argued that women should be denied the right to vote led a worship service at the Pentagon this week at the invitation of Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth.

Doug Wilson on Tuesday delivered a 15-minute sermon as part of a monthly Christian worship series started by Hegseth at the Defense Department this past May. In his sermon, which was broadcast live on the Pentagon’s internal TV network, Wilson said he prayed for “a black swan revival,” or another great awakening of Christianity in the country.

“God can do what he likes, and as we should know by now, what he likes to do is to take the most unlikely materials and do something glorious with it,” Wilson said to an auditorium of military personnel. “Take a prayer meeting at the Pentagon for a possible example. Many stranger things have happened. God is great.”

A DOD account on social media later posted a photo of Hegseth praying with his hand on Wilson’s shoulder and stating: “We have gathered at the Pentagon for our monthly worship service. We are One Nation Under God.”

Hegseth is the Trump administration’s most prominent member of Wilson’s church — the Communion of Reformed Evangelical Churches — which was founded in Moscow, Idaho, in the 1970s and has grown into a network of more than 150 churches internationally. Among Wilson’s professed beliefs is that homosexuality should be a crime, women should submit to their husbands, the 19th Amendment granting women the right to vote should be repealed, and Christians who owned slaves in the South “were on firm scriptural ground.” Women in the church are barred from leadership positions.

Wilson last year opened a church in Washington, D.C., and told CNN it was part of his plan to make the U.S. a Christian nation. Hegseth attends the church with his wife and children.

Wilson’s presence at the Pentagon quickly drew criticism that it is eroding the long-held U.S. tradition of separation of church and state.

“This is an unconstitutional and extreme attack on the 1st Amendment going completely unchecked by Congress. Hegseth is using his official position to make his religion the official one of the Department of Defense using official facilities, communications channels and personnel,” Fred Wellman, an Army veteran running for Congress in Missouri, wrote on X. “This must end and must be investigated.”

Defense officials, however, defended the invitation.

“Secretary Hegseth, along with millions of Americans, is a proud Christian and was glad to welcome Pastor Wilson to the Pentagon on Tuesday,” Pentagon press secretary Kingsley Wilson said in a statement to The Hill.

“The Christian faith is woven deeply into the fabric of our nation,” she added. “Despite the Left’s efforts to remove our Christian heritage from our great nation, Secretary Hegseth is among those who embrace it.” 

Hegseth has previously drawn condemnation for praising and amplifying Wilson, including last August when he retweeted the pastor.

Asked about it at the time, Wilson said Hegseth “very much appreciates many of Mr. Wilson’s writings and teachings,” but that he supports the 19th Amendment.

data.title: Stonewall Pride flag removal sparks lawsuit
data.description:
Several nonprofit groups jointly filed a lawsuit on Tuesday suing the National Park Service (NPS), the Department of the Interior and Interior Secretary Doug Burgum for the removal of the Pride flag at Manhattan’s Stonewall National Monument earlier this month before it returned to the site days later.

The plaintiffs include the Gilbert Baker Foundation, named after the late artist who created the Pride flag. The foundation’s president Charles Beal said that the flag’s removal erases the “history and the voices Stonewall honors.”

“Stonewall is the birthplace of the modern LGBTQ+ rights movement,” Beal said in a statement. “The Pride flag is recognized globally as a symbol of hope and liberation for the LGBTQ+ community whose efforts and resistance define this monument.”

Plaintiffs argued that the flag’s removal was “no careless mistake,” drawing the parallel to other flags not removed at NPS sites, including Confederate battle flags, which are displayed for “historic and interpretive purposes,” the suit states.

“The NPS does not prohibit sales of Confederate flag merchandise at gift shops located on its premises; instead, it has merely requested that its vendors voluntarily withdraw such items from the shops,” the suit reads. “In contrast, the NPS has ordered the removal of all products recognizing the identities of LGBTQ+ people from its gift shops.”

They argue that the flag’s removal at Stonewall “is the latest example in a long line of efforts by the Trump administration to target the LGBTQ+ community for discrimination and opprobrium.”

The Hill has reached out to the Department of the Interior and NPS for comment.

The NPS previously told news outlets that the flag’s removal was pursuant of the Trump administration’s guidance to restrict the agency to displaying mostly American flags or Interior Department flags, with some exceptions, such as flags of military branches.

Manhattan Borough President Brad Hoylman-Sigal said the flag’s removal followed a federal order to end all Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) policies in executive departments.

The flag’s brief absence was preceded by the removal of a reference to the transgender community from Stonewall’s website in February 2025.

Local leaders and several others rallied outside the Stonewall site last week and rehoisted the flag.

“If you can’t fly a Pride flag steps from Stonewall monument, at the national monument for LGBTQ liberation, where can you fly it?” Hoylman-Sigal asked, The Associated Press reported. “So, we put it back.”

Sen. Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) called the Trump administration’s removal of the flag “a deeply outrageous action that must be reversed, it’s an effing disgrace.”

The fight over Stonewall’s flag came after the NPS restored a slavery exhibit at the President’s House in Philadelphia. The exhibit details the history of enslaved people who lived at the home, including nine of former President George Washington’s slaves.

The exhibit was restored the day before U.S. District Judge Cynthia Rufe’s Friday deadline. She wrote in her ruling that the exhibit’s removal was reminiscent of George Orwell’s dystopian novel “1984.”

data.title: FIFA head dons Trump 'USA' hat at ceremony
data.description:
FIFA President Gianni Infantino sported a red “USA” hat during the inaugural meeting of President Trump’s Board of Peace on Thursday as he pledged the governing body’s monetary support for rebuilding soccer infrastructure in Gaza.   

“We don’t have to just rebuild houses or schools or hospitals or roads. We also have to rebuild and build people[‘s] emotion, hope, and trust, and this is what football, my sport, is about,” Infantino told the room full of world leaders.

It was announced in a subsequent video that FIFA would invest tens of millions of dollars into building 50 mini pitches, five full-sized pitches in five different districts, a soccer academy, as well as a $50 million national stadium with a 20,000 to 25,0000 seat capacity.

“These investments will create a complex football ecosystem designed to support communities and future generations. FIFA and the Board of Peace: United for football, committed to peace,” a narrator states in the video.

The video featured AI-generated images of stadiums, fields, players, and fans among the rubble in Gaza.

Infantino has been cozying up to Trump in the lead-up to the 2026 World Cup, which will feature 78 matches across 11 U.S. cities, including all matches from the quarterfinal through to the final at MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford, N.J.

Trump was honored with the inaugural FIFA Peace Prize during the World Cup draw in December, an accolade FIFA said is bestowed upon “individuals who have taken exceptional and extraordinary actions for peace and by doing so have united people across the world.”

The president said at the time that it was “truly one of the great honors of my life” to win the prize.

The move was widely criticized as a transparent attempt at appeasing Trump, who had publicly lobbied for the Nobel Peace Prize before it was awarded to Venezuelan opposition leader María Corina Machado. 

Machado later gave her prize medal to Trump, though the Norwegian Nobel Committee said the actual peace prize could not be transferred.

FIFA’s prize to Trump sparked formal complaints to FIFA’s ethics investigators, including one from a London-based human rights nonprofit that argued Infantino’s actions violated the body’s policy mandating that officials remain politically neutral, according to the Associated Press.

That policy was referenced by some users in social media posts reacting to Infantino’s accessory choice at Thursday’s meeting.

Trump is chairing the Board of Peace, which is tasked with supervising postwar restoration efforts in Gaza, including the disarmament of Hamas, as part of the second phase of the president’s 20-point peace plan.

On Thursday, Trump praised both the world leaders who had accepted invitations to join and Infantino.

“This is the most prestigious board ever put together. You know, I’ve seen some great corporate boards. I’ve seen some great boards, period. It’s peanuts compared to this board because we have… virtually everyone is the head of a country other than Johnny, but he’s the head of soccer, so that’s not so bad, right, Johnny? I like your job the best, I think,” he said.

data.title: Trump on Ocasio-Cortez's Munich response: 'I didn't know she was stupid'
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday criticized Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.) again for her remarks at the Munich Security Conference last weekend.

“Her performance was horrible. I was surprised actually, I didn’t know she was stupid,” he told reporters aboard Air Force One en route to Rome, Ga. 

The president specifically pointed to her foreign policy remarks as a potentially “career-ending” answer. 

Ocasio-Cortez sidestepped a question about whether U.S. troops would back Taiwan if it was invaded by China. The New York lawmaker said she hopes “we never get to that point, and we want to make sure that we are moving in all of our economic research and our global positions to avoid any such confrontation.”

Trump said he would have responded differently. 

“I would’ve just said ‘it’s none of your business right now.’ Because I wouldn’t answer that question. I wouldn’t respond to a question like that,” he told reporters.

“It’s war. When it comes to war you don’t talk about your strategies. She could have done that briefly. She had no idea. I don’t think she knew what Taiwan was,” he added.

Others, including Democrats and Republicans, have criticized Ocasio-Cortez for her performance on the world stage. In addition to critiques of her response to foreign policy questions, she mistakenly referred to Venezuela as a country located “below the equator.” 

Vice President Vance said the incident was “embarrassing.”

“If I had given that answer I would say, ‘You know what? Maybe you ought to go read a book about China and Taiwan before I go out on the world stage again,’” he said during a Tuesday appearance on Fox News’s “The Story.”

data.title: Fine to introduce Protecting Puppies from Sharia Act amid criticism over post
data.description:
Rep. Randy Fine (R-Fla.) is set to introduce a bill in support of dog ownership as he faces a wave of criticism over a social media post he made about choosing dogs over Muslims.

The brief bill, titled the Protecting Puppies from Sharia Act, would block federal funds from flowing to any state or local government that prohibits residents from owning dogs. It’s not clear if any such jurisdictions exist, though some areas do have breed restrictions.

A nonbinding “sense of Congress” section states that “Sharia law is a foreign concept that is against the pursuit of happiness” and that “it is a right of all Americans to own a dog.” Text of the bill was first shared with The Hill.

It is Fine’s latest act of defiance as high-profile Democrats call on the Florida congressman to resign over his “Islamophobic” post, or to be formally censured by the House and removed from committee assignments.

“All the Democrats have criticized me, every single one of them, not a single one has said, ‘I promise I will never let anyone take your dogs away.’ You know why? Because they don’t want to offend those who do what,” Fine said in a phone interview with The Hill on Thursday.

Fine’s social media post from Sunday read: “If they force us to choose, the choice between dogs and Muslims is not a difficult one.”

He later followed up with context, posting a screenshot of a post from pro-Palestinian activist Nerdeen Kiswani from last week in which she said: “Finally, NYC is coming to Islam. Dogs definitely have a place in society, just not as indoor pets. Like we’ve said all along, they are unclean.” 

Kiswani has since said the post was a joke as a reference to melting snow exposing dog waste, but Fine said he did not believe that. 

“There’s no reading of it that sounds satirical. There’s no, no reasonable person can say, ‘Ha ha, this is funny,’” Fine said. “In Islam, they don’t like dogs, and I’m not criticizing their religion for believing that — you know, some in my religion, we don’t eat pork. … So when a major Muslim leader says we’ve got to get rid of them, you should take them seriously.”

His post quickly went viral, drawing sharp criticism from Fine’s Democratic colleagues in the House and beyond.

House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) in a statement on Tuesday called Fine “a disgrace to the United States Congress” and “an Islamaphobic, disgusting bigot,” calling on Republicans to “hold this so-called Member of Congress accountable.”

Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (N.Y.) was one of many Democrats to call for Fine to be formally censured by the House and stripped of committees, and California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) said that Fine should resign. 

Fine, however, dug in on his comments — responding to critics with modified Gadsden flags that depicted puppies instead of a snake while proclaiming, “Don’t tread on me.” 

Fine told The Hill he differentiated between what he calls “mainstream Islam” and “radical Islam,” and that his post did not mean he would ban Islam as a religion.

“If a group of people says, ‘Hey, we’ve just shown up in your country, and dogs violate our religion, so you have to stop having them.’ My answer to them is, ‘No, these are our pets. You can go home,’” Fine said. “Immigration means assimilation. Immigration doesn’t mean the people that were there have to change how they live to make you happy.”

And Fine teased: “Wait until you see who my guest is for the State of the Union.”
